entries.title: ShipLog
entries.description: A personal changelog of everything you've shipped. Auto-generated from GitHub activity across all your orgs. The journal you did know you were writing.

entries.title: Gravity-Garden
entries.description: N-body gravity sandbox. Place stars, watch orbits form, collisions merge mass. Tilt on mobile. Single HTML file.

entries.title: Starfield
entries.description: A 3D first-person starfield flight game. Tilt your phone or use your keyboard. Dodge asteroids, chase the void. Successor to a 1990 starfield simulator.

entries.title: WarpField
entries.description: Interactive dot wave field visualization. Move your mouse, change the pattern, lose yourself.

entries.title: GitWell
entries.description: CLI tool that recovers abandoned intentions from your git history — stale branches, forgotten stashes, orphaned commits, and unfinished work. Your git history is a journal you didn't know you were writing.

entries.title: Starfield
entries.description: A 3D first-person starfield flight game. Tilt your phone or use your keyboard. Dodge asteroids, chase the void. Successor to a 1990 starfield simulator.

entries.title: Aftermark
entries.description: Browser extension that treats bookmarks as compressed intentions, not passive links. Indexes locally, infers why you saved things, clusters saves into projects and decisions, and helps you act on what you meant to come back to. Local-first, privacy-respecting. Your bookmarks are breadcrumbs — Aftermark helps you follow the trail.

entries.title: signal
entries.description: A tiny Rust CLI for turning moods into action. Add suggestions, get random nudges, and avoid repetition with LRU-biased selection. Fast, local, and built for real-life momentum.

entries.title: WISP
entries.description: Lisp in WASM

entries.title: unit
entries.description: A self-replicating software nanobot. Forth interpreter + S-expression mesh protocol + genetic programming + distributed computation + persistence, all in one binary. Units spawn copies, evolve their own code, distribute goals across peers, hibernate and resurrect. Works across machines over UDP. Try the live demo.

entries.title: SparkChoice
entries.description: SparkChoice helps AI choose the next best action. It evaluates candidate moves, scores them for leverage and readiness, selects one concrete artifact-producing step, and explains why that choice beats the alternatives.

entries.title: Thinking-the-Unthinkable
entries.description: How to use AI as a cognitive lever for genuinely novel thinking — breaking out of mental ruts, exploring alien perspectives, and reaching creative territory your brain can't access alone. All the mind-expansion, none of the acid.

entries.title: Drinking-From-the-Firehose
entries.description: A practical guide to leveraging AI for information triage without falling into filter bubbles. Covers attention economics, AI-assisted relevance filtering, the dangers of non-adversarial algorithmic curation, and the underrated art of strategic selective ignorance.

entries.title: The-Agony-of-Consensus-Algorithms
entries.description: Paxos, Raft, PBFT, Viewstamped Replication, and more — compared and contrasted with brutal honesty. Covers failure models, FLP impossibility, classic and modern consensus protocols, Byzantine fault tolerance, and when to use what. Stop copying what Kafka does and actually understand the tradeoffs.

entries.title: Understanding-CORS
entries.description: A no-nonsense guide to Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — the web security mechanism everyone encounters and nobody fully understands. Covers the Same-Origin Policy, preflight requests, credential handling, and every header you'll ever need. Stop copy-pasting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and actually learn what's happening.

entries.title: Knowledge-Management
entries.description: A rigorous guide to knowledge theory and management — from epistemological foundations through organizational KM practices to the modern frontier. Explores how AI transforms knowledge capture, retrieval, and synthesis, and how anyone can build powerful offline searchable knowledge bases with today's tools.

entries.title: Event-Driven-Architecture
entries.description: A deep dive into event-driven architecture patterns, followed by a thorough comparison of event brokers — from mainstream heavyweights like Kafka, RabbitMQ, and Pulsar to more obscure options like ZeroMQ, NATS, and Memphis. Covers trade-offs in throughput, latency, durability, and operational complexity.

entries.title: impossibility-theorems
entries.description: A deep dive into the fundamental limits of computation, logic, and systems — Gödel, Turing, Arrow, CAP, and beyond. Proven impossibilities and open conjectures that every engineer and thinker should know.

entries.title: v0.2.0 — SQLite persistence and signed JWT delegated tokens
entries.description: AIPassport is an exploratory reference implementation of OAuth-inspired delegated AI access. It lets users grant third-party apps scoped, time-limited, revocable access to their AI providers — without ever exposing raw API keys.  ### What's in this release  **SQLite persistence.** Grants, tokens,...

entries.title: AIPassport
entries.description: OAuth-inspired delegated AI access. A broker that lets users grant third-party apps scoped, time-limited, revocable access to AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) without exposing raw API keys. Exploratory reference implementation with SQLite persistence and signed JWT tokens.

entries.title: repo-mood
entries.description: Read the emotional state of any public GitHub repo. Feeds real signals — commit velocity, open issues, PR pressure, branch sprawl — to an AI that synthesizes a mood reading and color. Built with vanilla JS + Claude.

entries.title: Postgres-is-Enough
entries.description: A love letter to PostgreSQL — exploring how far you can push it as a relational DB, document store, job queue, key-value store, vector database, and beyond. With the right extensions, postgres is enough.

entries.title: HTTP-Alternatives-for-APIs
entries.description: Survey of API communication protocols beyond HTTP — gRPC, MQTT, AMQP, ZeroMQ, Cap'n Proto RPC, and more. Covers tradeoffs, pseudoalternatives, and how to choose.

entries.title: Systems-Thinking
entries.description: Systems thinking predates writing itself — humans have always reasoned about wholes, not just parts. This book traces that lineage from cybernetics and Forrester's world models to modern complexity science, digital twins, and AI-assisted simulation. What does it mean to think in systems in 2026, and why does it matter more than ever?

entries.title: Asking-the-Right-Questions
entries.description: A practical guide to question-driven thinking — how to find the questions worth asking, why most people never do, and how to develop the habit of asking what nobody else thought to ask.

entries.title: High-Performance-Computing-in-2026
entries.description: HPC for the modern era — GPU computing, CUDA vs ROCm vs Metal, MPI, OpenMP, vectorization, and the intersection with ML workloads. Practical for engineers, not just academics.

entries.title: Post-Quantum-Cryptography
entries.description: A developer's guide to cryptography in the post-quantum era — covering lattice-based algorithms, NIST standards like CRYSTALS-Kyber and Dilithium, and what "harvest now, decrypt later" means for systems you're building today.

entries.title: Q-in-the-Wild
entries.description: kdb+/q is powerful but lives in a silo. This book tears down the walls — integrating Q with Rust, Python, and R, finding the IDE plugins that actually work, and connecting kdb+/q to web frameworks and mainstream tooling. For the Q developer who refuses to be an island.

entries.title: Reinforcement-Learning-with-Q
entries.description: Reinforcement Learning concepts from Sutton & Barto's canonical text, implemented in kdb+/q. Bandits, dynamic programming, TD learning, and Q-learning — all in the language built for high-performance data. The book Dmitriy wished existed when he started.

entries.title: Distributed-Systems-for-the-Rest-of-Us
entries.description: CAP theorem, eventual consistency, replication, and fault tolerance — explained for engineers who aren't at Google scale but need to think like they are. No hand-waving, no oversimplification. Just the mental models that make distributed systems decisions less terrifying.

entries.title: Backend-Rust
entries.description: Rust on the server

entries.title: Build-Your-Own-AI
entries.description: Roll your own

entries.title: The-Zero-Build-Movement
entries.description: How far can you go without a build system?

entries.title: lemonsqueezy.js
entries.description: Fork of the Official JavaScript SDK for Lemon Squeezy.

entries.title: slarchive
entries.description: Don't get trapped inside a weird export format from Slack

entries.title: The-Open-Source-Intelligence-Bible
entries.description: OSINT in 2026

entries.title: Your-Tools-Are-Lying-to-You
entries.description: By Claude Code Sonnet 4.6

entries.title: The-Big-Book-of-Compression-Algorithms
entries.description: Everything compresses differently. A survey of the major algorithms — Huffman to Zstandard, lossless to lossy, zip files to video codecs — covering mechanics, tradeoffs, and ideal contexts. Closes with a bonus section on why LLMs are, at their core, just really expensive lossy compressors.

entries.title: Rack-Roda-and-Ruby
entries.description: Rails is just a callable. Sinatra is just a callable. So is Roda. This book strips away the magic of Ruby web development, shows you exactly what Rack is doing under the hood, and teaches you to build from first principles. Know your tools. Really know them.

entries.title: Back-to-Bare-Metal-with-Python-WSGI-and-ASGI
entries.description: Django and FastAPI are just fancy callables. This book strips away the magic, shows you exactly what's happening under the hood, and teaches you to build Python web apps from first principles — synchronous with WSGI, async with ASGI. Know your tools. Really know them.

entries.title: You-Are-Not-Your-Stack
entries.description: Your language doesn't define you. Your framework won't save you. Your stack will be obsolete before you retire. This book is about what remains when you strip all of that away — and how to build a career and identity that survives every technology shift that's coming.

entries.title: Let-Erlang-Crash
entries.description: Erlang was built to never die. Ironically, the secret is letting everything crash. This book embraces the chaos, explores the BEAM, and makes fault-tolerant programming genuinely fun.

entries.title: gitorg-mcp
entries.description: An MCP server that exposes your GitHub organization data to AI assistants. Query repos, find stale projects, check issues, and view stats across all your orgs — directly from Claude, VS Code, or any MCP-compatible host. Built in Rust. Powered by gitorg.

entries.title: MCP-Model-Context-Protocol
entries.description: A developer's guide to the Model Context Protocol — the open standard connecting AI apps to tools and data. Covers architecture, wire protocol, building servers (TypeScript & Python) and clients, all 10 SDKs, configuration, security, testing, production patterns, and the ecosystem.

entries.title: gitorg
entries.description: CLI that gives you a bird's-eye view across all your GitHub orgs. Repo counts, recent commits, stale repos, open issues.

entries.title: STEAM-and-Autism
entries.description: Special considerations for autistic STEAM students

entries.title: macOS-and-Unix
entries.description: All about Unix features in your Mac

entries.title: vibedetector
entries.description: Detect whether tell-tale files are in a folder, including CLAUDE.md, windsurf, cursor, etc.

entries.title: Networking-Protocols
entries.description: Comprehensive guide to networking protocols covering TCP/IP stack, HTTP evolution (1.0 through HTTP/3), WebSockets, QUIC, UDP vs TCP, DNS resolution, TLS/SSL handshakes, and modern protocol design. Includes packet flow diagrams, performance characteristics, and real-world use cases.

entries.title: Database-Internals
entries.description: Ever wondered what happens when you hit COMMIT? Journey into the storage engines, B-trees, WAL logs, and MVCC magic powering modern databases. From disk I/O to query optimization, learn how databases turn SQL into bits. Written by Opus 4.5 for developers who want to understand why their queries are slow.

entries.title: RubyFruit
entries.description: RubyFruit is an educational Ruby project that teaches core Object-Oriented Programming concepts. It features a base Fruit class with subclasses like Apple, Banana, and Orange, demonstrating inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and modules—written with clear comments for beginners.

entries.title: clitracker
entries.description: CLITracker is a Rust-based terminal productivity tool with an ncurses UI. It integrates with Pivotal Tracker’s API to fetch, update, and manage tasks from your command line. Designed for developers who live in the terminal, it keeps you focused and efficient without switching to a browser.

entries.title: stellar-core
entries.description: stellar-core is the backbone of the Stellar network. It maintains a local copy of the ledger, communicating and staying in sync with other instances of stellar-core on the network. Optionally, stellar-core can store historical records of the ledger and participate in consensus.
